scope_note: Contiguous indexed collections with O(1) random access; covers static and dynamic (resizable) variants but not linked structures.
Node-and-pointer sequential structures (singly, doubly, circular); distinct from arrays in insertion/deletion trade-offs.
LIFO (stack) and FIFO (queue) abstract data types including deques; distinct from message queues (performance_scalability).
Key-value stores using hash functions for O(1) average lookup; covers collision strategies but not cryptographic hashing (security).
Trees where each node has at most two children; covers BSTs and traversals but not self-balancing variants (balanced_search_trees).
Self-balancing tree structures (AVL, red-black, B-tree) that guarantee O(log n) operations; distinct from storage-engine B-trees (databases).
Tree-based priority queue structures supporting efficient min/max extraction; distinct from memory heap allocation (operating_systems).
Vertices and edges as a data structure; covers representations (adjacency list/matrix) but not specific traversal algorithms.
Prefix-tree structures for efficient string storage and retrieval; distinct from full-text search indices (databases).
Disjoint-set data structure with union and find operations; key for Kruskal's MST and connected-component queries.
Probabilistic set-membership structures with no false negatives; distinct from general hashing or caching strategies.
Asymptotic analysis of time and space requirements (Big-O, Omega, Theta); covers amortized analysis but not benchmarking (performance_scalability).
Self-referential function calls with base cases; foundational to divide-and-conquer, DP, and tree traversals.
Comparison and non-comparison sorting methods (merge, quick, heap, radix, counting); covers stability and in-place properties.
Methods for finding elements in collections (linear, binary, interpolation); distinct from graph search (graph_traversal) and text search (string_algorithms).
Systematic exploration of graph vertices via BFS or DFS; foundational for shortest-path, topological sort, and cycle detection.
Algorithms finding minimum-weight paths between vertices (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, Floyd-Warshall, A*); distinct from network routing protocols (networking).
Subset of edges connecting all vertices with minimum total weight (Kruskal's, Prim's); classic greedy algorithm application.
Linear ordering of DAG vertices respecting edge direction; used in build systems, task scheduling, and dependency resolution.
Optimization by breaking problems into overlapping subproblems with memoization or tabulation; distinct from greedy (no backtrack) approaches.
Algorithms making locally optimal choices at each step; covers correctness proofs (exchange arguments, matroids) and when greedy fails.
Recursive decomposition into independent subproblems (merge sort, quicksort, Strassen); uses the master theorem for analysis.
Systematic exploration with pruning for constraint-satisfaction and combinatorial problems; distinct from brute-force enumeration.
Pattern matching and text processing algorithms (KMP, Rabin-Karp, suffix arrays/trees); distinct from regex engines (programming_languages).
Non-cryptographic hash functions for data structures (collision handling, universal hashing); cryptographic hashing lives in security.
Using bitwise operators (AND, OR, XOR, shifts) for efficient computation and compact state representation.
Complexity classes (P, NP, NP-complete, NP-hard), reductions, and intractability; distinct from runtime analysis of specific algorithms.
Max-flow/min-cut algorithms (Ford-Fulkerson, Edmonds-Karp) on flow networks; distinct from network routing or data flow in systems.
Algorithms using random choices for correctness or efficiency (Monte Carlo, Las Vegas, randomized quicksort, skip lists).
OS-managed execution units with their own address space; distinct from threads (concurrency) which share address space within a process.
How the OS decides which process/thread runs next on the CPU; covers context switching mechanics but not specific policies.
OS-level synchronization primitives and critical-section management; language-level concurrency primitives live in concurrency domain.
OS-level memory allocation, paging, and segmentation; distinct from language-level garbage collection (programming_languages) and memory models (concurrency).
Address space abstraction via page tables, TLB, and demand paging; distinct from virtual address space concepts in programming.
On-disk data organization (inodes, directories, journaling); distinct from distributed file systems and object storage (devops_infrastructure).
OS I/O subsystem including device drivers, DMA, buffering, and spooling; distinct from application-level async I/O (concurrency).
Interface between user-space programs and the kernel; covers the trap mechanism and common syscall families (file, process, network).
Mechanisms for processes to exchange data (pipes, shared memory, message queues, sockets); distinct from distributed messaging (performance_scalability).
Kernel design choices (monolithic, micro, hybrid, exokernel) and their trade-offs; distinct from software architecture patterns.
Hardware and software interrupt mechanisms including interrupt vectors, handlers, and priority schemes.
System startup sequence from firmware (BIOS/UEFI) through bootloader to kernel initialization and init process.
Specific scheduling algorithms and policies (FCFS, RR, priority, CFS, MLFQ); builds on cpu_scheduling mechanics.
OS-level protection mechanisms (rings, capabilities, access control lists); distinct from application-level security (security domain).
Hardware-level virtualization via hypervisors (Type 1/2); distinct from containers (devops_infrastructure) which share the host kernel.
Algorithms for ordering disk I/O requests (FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN); mostly relevant to spinning disks.
Mapping device registers or files into the process address space via mmap; distinct from port-mapped I/O.
Asynchronous notifications sent to processes (SIGTERM, SIGKILL, SIGUSR); distinct from event-driven programming patterns.
Programming organized around procedures/functions with sequential execution; the baseline paradigm before OOP and FP.
Paradigm based on objects, classes, inheritance, and polymorphism; distinct from design patterns (architecture) which are OOP applications.
Paradigm emphasizing pure functions, immutability, and composition; distinct from the mathematical foundation (lambda_calculus).
Classification of values into types and enforcement rules (static vs dynamic, strong vs weak, structural vs nominal).
Automatic deduction of types without explicit annotations (Hindley-Milner, local inference, bidirectional); distinct from type checking.
Parameterized types enabling code reuse across types (Java generics, C++ templates, Rust generics); covers type erasure vs reification.
Composite types formed by sum (variants/enums) and product (structs/tuples) operations; foundational for pattern matching.
Structural decomposition and conditional branching on data shapes; distinct from regex pattern matching (string_algorithms).
Functions that capture variables from their enclosing scope; distinct from anonymous functions that don't capture state.
How a language organizes memory (stack, heap, static); distinct from OS-level memory management and concurrency memory models.
Automatic memory reclamation strategies (mark-sweep, generational, reference counting); distinct from manual memory management and ownership.
Compile-time memory safety via ownership rules (Rust's borrow checker, C++ move semantics, RAII); alternative to GC.
Language-level error handling mechanisms (exceptions, Result/Option types, error codes); distinct from API error responses (api_design).
Language-level concurrency abstractions (green threads, goroutines, virtual threads); distinct from OS-level threading and concurrency primitives.
Rules for variable visibility and name resolution (lexical vs dynamic scoping, shadowing, hoisting).
Translating source code to machine code via lexing, parsing, optimization, and code generation; distinct from build systems (software_construction).
Executing code directly or via bytecode (tree-walking, bytecode VM, JIT); covers the spectrum from pure interpretation to JIT compilation.
Programs that generate or transform other programs (macros, reflection, compile-time code generation); distinct from runtime code eval.
Abstraction mechanisms for defining shared behavior (Java interfaces, Rust traits, Haskell typeclasses, Swift protocols).
How subtyping between parameterized types relates to subtyping of their type arguments (covariant, contravariant, invariant).
Language-level code organization into reusable units (modules, packages, namespaces); distinct from package managers (software_construction).
Formal mathematical foundation of computation using function abstraction and application; the theory underlying functional programming.
Type-system tracking of computational side effects (monads, algebraic effects, effect handlers); research frontier in PL design.
Organizing code into horizontal layers (presentation, business, data access) with unidirectional dependencies.
Decomposing applications into independently deployable services; distinct from SOA in its emphasis on autonomy and decentralized governance.
Single-deployment-unit application architecture; not inherently bad — covers when monoliths are appropriate.
Monolith with strong module boundaries that could be split into services later; middle ground between monolith and microservices.
Architecture where components communicate through events; distinct from event_sourcing (storing state as events) and pub_sub (messaging pattern).
Ports-and-adapters pattern isolating domain logic from infrastructure; closely related to but predates clean architecture.
Concentric-circle architecture with dependency rule pointing inward; synthesizes hexagonal, onion, and screaming architecture ideas.
Modeling software around business domains with ubiquitous language, aggregates, and entities; strategic (bounded contexts) and tactical (entities, value objects) patterns.
Explicit boundary within which a domain model applies; each bounded context has its own ubiquitous language.
Separating read and write models for independent optimization; often paired with event sourcing but not required.
Persisting state as an append-only sequence of domain events rather than current state; distinct from event-driven architecture (communication pattern).
Fundamental design quality metrics: low coupling between modules and high cohesion within them.
Five OO design principles (SRP, OCP, LSP, ISP, DIP) guiding maintainable, extensible code; distinct from design patterns which are solutions.
Providing dependencies to components rather than having them create their own; key technique for testability and loose coupling.
Reusable solutions to common OO design problems (factory, observer, strategy, etc.); distinct from architectural patterns.
Abstracting data access behind a collection-like interface; separates domain logic from persistence details.
Organizing code by feature/use-case cutting through all layers, rather than by horizontal layer; reduces cross-feature coupling.
Coarse-grained services communicating via an enterprise service bus; precursor to microservices with more centralized governance.
Messaging pattern where publishers emit events to topics and subscribers consume them; distinct from point-to-point queues.
Incrementally replacing a legacy system by routing traffic to new implementations; named after the strangler fig tree.
Translation layer preventing one system's model from corrupting another's; key DDD pattern at bounded context boundaries.
Dedicated backend service tailored to a specific frontend client's needs; distinct from a general-purpose API gateway.
Methodology for building SaaS apps (codebase, dependencies, config, backing services, etc.); foundational for cloud-native design.
Data processing through a chain of independent filter stages connected by pipes; Unix philosophy applied to software design.
Co-deploying a helper process alongside the main service for cross-cutting concerns (logging, proxying); foundation of service mesh.
Architecture distributing processing and data across in-memory grids to eliminate database bottlenecks at extreme scale.
Event-driven compute where cloud providers manage servers and scaling; covers architectural implications, not just deployment.
Architectural style using HTTP methods on resources with stateless communication; distinct from the HTTP protocol itself (networking).
Query language for APIs allowing clients to request exactly the data they need; alternative to REST with different trade-offs.
High-performance RPC framework using HTTP/2 and protocol buffers; distinct from REST in being contract-first and binary.
Strategies for evolving APIs without breaking clients (URL path, header, query param versioning); covers deprecation policies.
Breaking large result sets into pages (offset, cursor, keyset); covers API-level pagination distinct from database cursors.
Constraining API request frequency per client/key using token bucket, sliding window, etc.; distinct from general throttling (performance_scalability).
Mechanism for client and server to agree on response format (JSON, XML, etc.) via HTTP Accept headers.

aliases: hypermedia as the engine of application state
scope_note: REST maturity level 3 where responses include hypermedia links enabling client-driven navigation; rarely implemented in practice.
Single entry point handling cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, routing) for backend services; distinct from reverse proxy (networking).
Server-to-server HTTP callbacks triggered by events; push-based alternative to polling.
Standardized error response formats and HTTP status code conventions for APIs (RFC 7807 Problem Details); distinct from language-level error handling.
Machine-readable API description format (formerly Swagger); enables code generation, documentation, and testing.
Authentication mechanisms specific to APIs (API keys, bearer tokens, mutual TLS); distinct from user-facing authentication (security).
API patterns for long-running operations (polling, callbacks, server-sent events); distinct from language-level async/await.
Data interchange formats used in APIs (JSON, XML, Protocol Buffers, MessagePack); distinct from storage-level serialization.
Designing API resources and their URIs to reflect domain entities and relationships; foundational REST design skill.
Conventions for filtering, sorting, and selecting fields in API responses via query parameters.
API patterns for creating, updating, or deleting multiple resources in a single request; covers partial failure semantics.
Designing client libraries that wrap APIs with idiomatic language bindings, retry logic, and type safety.
Verifying user or system identity (who you are); distinct from authorization (what you can do).
Determining what an authenticated entity is allowed to do; distinct from authentication (identity verification).
Delegated authorization framework with flows for web, mobile, and server-to-server; distinct from OpenID Connect which adds authentication.
Identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0 providing authentication via ID tokens; distinct from OAuth 2.0 which handles authorization only.
Compact, self-contained token format for securely transmitting claims; covers signing (JWS) and encryption (JWE).
Managing user sessions (creation, expiration, invalidation, storage); covers security concerns like fixation and hijacking.
Encryption using a single shared key (AES, ChaCha20); distinct from public-key cryptography which uses key pairs.
Encryption using public/private key pairs (RSA, ECC); enables key exchange, digital signatures, and TLS.
Cryptographic one-way functions (SHA-256, bcrypt, Argon2) for integrity and password storage; distinct from non-crypto hashing (algorithms_data_structures).
Protocol securing communication via encryption, authentication, and integrity (TLS 1.2/1.3); distinct from the HTTP protocol it wraps.
Managing X.509 certificates including issuance, rotation, revocation, and PKI trust chains.
Injecting malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users; prevented by output encoding and CSP.
Tricking a browser into making authenticated requests to a different site; prevented by tokens and SameSite cookies.
Injecting malicious SQL through unsanitized input; prevented by parameterized queries and ORM usage.
Validating and sanitizing all external input before processing; foundational defense against injection attacks.
Browser mechanism controlling cross-origin HTTP requests; relaxes the same-origin policy in a controlled manner.
HTTP header restricting which resources a page can load; primary defense against XSS and data injection attacks.
Securely storing and distributing secrets (API keys, passwords, certificates) using vaults and rotation; distinct from key management (cryptographic keys).
Access control model assigning permissions to roles rather than individuals; distinct from ABAC (attribute-based) and ReBAC (relationship-based).
Systematic identification of security threats and mitigations (STRIDE, DREAD, attack trees); distinct from penetration testing (active exploitation).
Security model where no entity is trusted by default regardless of network location; continuous verification of every request.
Securing the software supply chain from source to deployment (SBOMs, signing, provenance, dependency scanning).
Models for controlling resource access (ACL, ABAC, ReBAC); broader category that includes RBAC as one approach.
Vulnerabilities from improper configuration (default creds, verbose errors, unnecessary services); OWASP Top 10 category.
Practices for writing code that resists attacks (input validation, output encoding, least privilege); overlaps with defensive_programming but security-focused.
Requiring multiple verification factors (something you know, have, are); covers TOTP, WebAuthn, and push-based methods.
Cryptographic proof of authenticity and integrity using private key signing and public key verification.
Lifecycle management of cryptographic keys (generation, distribution, rotation, revocation); distinct from secrets management (non-key secrets).
Logging security-relevant events for audit and detection (login attempts, access changes, anomalies); distinct from application logging.
Authorized simulated attacks to find vulnerabilities before adversaries do; distinct from threat modeling (design-time) and automated scanning.
Quantitative measure of service behavior (latency, availability, error rate) that feeds into SLOs.
Target reliability level for an SLI (e.g., 99.9% availability); SLAs are contractual commitments based on SLOs.
Allowed unreliability (1 - SLO) that balances feature velocity with reliability; when exhausted, focus shifts to stability.
Emitting logs as structured key-value pairs (JSON) for machine parsing; distinct from unstructured text logs.
Collecting numerical measurements over time (counters, gauges, histograms); covers Prometheus, OpenTelemetry, and custom metrics.
Tracking requests across service boundaries via trace IDs and spans; the third pillar of observability alongside logs and metrics.
Automated notification when metrics breach thresholds or SLOs are at risk; covers alert design to avoid fatigue.
Endpoints verifying service health (liveness, readiness, startup probes); used by load balancers and orchestrators for routing decisions.
Deliberately introducing failures to test system resilience (Chaos Monkey, Litmus); distinct from load testing (testing domain).
Maintaining partial functionality when components fail rather than total outage; covers fallback strategies and feature toggles.
Preventing cascading failures by short-circuiting calls to failing services (closed/open/half-open states).
Retrying failed operations with increasing delays and jitter to avoid thundering herd; requires idempotent operations.
Isolating failures by partitioning resources (thread pools, connection pools) so one failing component doesn't consume all resources.
Process for detecting, triaging, and resolving production incidents with defined roles and communication channels.
Blameless review of incidents to identify root causes, contributing factors, and preventive actions; distinct from code review.
Engineers available to respond to production issues on a rotation schedule; covers escalation policies and handoff procedures.
Step-by-step operational procedures for common tasks and incident response; candidate for automation.
Identifying and eliminating repetitive, manual operational work that scales linearly with service growth.
Designing systems that continue operating correctly despite component failures; umbrella concept for circuit breakers, retries, and redundancy.
Infrastructure for collecting, processing, and routing telemetry data (OTel Collector, Vector, Fluentd); distinct from the signals themselves.
Simulating user interactions to proactively detect issues before real users are affected; distinct from real user monitoring (RUM).
Patterns for storing frequently accessed data closer to consumers (cache-aside, write-through, write-back); distinct from invalidation strategies.
Strategies for keeping cached data consistent with source of truth (TTL, event-based, version-based); famously one of the two hard problems.
Adding more machines/instances to handle increased load; requires stateless design or distributed state management.
Increasing resources (CPU, RAM, disk) on a single machine; simpler but has upper limits.
Distributing traffic across multiple servers using algorithms (round-robin, least-connections, consistent hashing); covers L4 vs L7.
Automatically adjusting compute resources based on demand metrics; covers scaling policies, cooldown periods, and predictive scaling.
Geographically distributed caching network for static and dynamic content; distinct from application-level caching.
Limiting the rate of processing to prevent overload; broader than API rate limiting, applies to any processing pipeline.
Analyzing program execution to identify performance bottlenecks (CPU, memory, I/O profiling, flame graphs); distinct from benchmarking.
Measuring performance under controlled conditions to establish baselines and compare alternatives; distinct from profiling (finding bottlenecks).
Forecasting future resource needs based on growth patterns and performance data to prevent capacity-related outages.
Deferring resource loading until actually needed to reduce initial load time; applies to images, code, data, and database relations.
Offloading work to background workers via job queues for non-blocking request handling; distinct from language-level async/await.
Infrastructure for asynchronous message passing between services (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS); distinct from pub_sub (pattern) and IPC (OS-level).
Optimizing database connection pool sizes, timeouts, and configuration for throughput and latency; distinct from query optimization.
Automated build, test, and deployment pipelines; CI merges frequently, CD automates release to production.
Managing infrastructure through declarative or imperative code (Terraform, Pulumi, CDK); distinct from configuration management (runtime config).
Packaging applications with their dependencies in isolated containers (Docker, OCI); distinct from VMs which include full OS.
Automated management of containerized workloads (scheduling, scaling, networking, self-healing); Kubernetes is the dominant platform.
Running two identical production environments and switching traffic atomically; enables instant rollback.
Gradually rolling out changes to a subset of users/servers before full deployment; enables data-driven release decisions.
Updating instances incrementally one at a time to maintain availability; default strategy in Kubernetes.
Using Git as the single source of truth for declarative infrastructure and application state; pull-based deployment model.
Infrastructure layer handling service-to-service communication (mTLS, retries, routing) via sidecar proxies; distinct from API gateway.
Managing runtime configuration across environments (Ansible, Chef, Puppet); distinct from IaC which provisions infrastructure.
Runtime toggles controlling feature availability without deployment; enables trunk-based development and gradual rollouts.
Storing and versioning build artifacts (container images, packages, binaries); includes registries and retention policies.
Replacing servers entirely rather than modifying in place; eliminates configuration drift and enables reproducible deployments.
Managing multiple deployment environments (dev, staging, production) with parity; covers promotion workflows and environment-specific config.
Visual displays of system health metrics and KPIs (Grafana, Datadog); distinct from the metrics collection infrastructure itself.
Collecting and centralizing logs from distributed systems (ELK, Loki, Splunk); distinct from structured logging (format) and observability pipelines (transport).
Testing infrastructure code and configurations (Terratest, InSpec, Open Policy Agent); distinct from application testing.
Expressing governance and compliance rules as executable code (OPA/Rego, Sentinel); enables automated policy enforcement.
Fundamental cloud building blocks (compute, storage, networking, managed services); covers IaaS/PaaS/SaaS distinctions.
Development practice where all developers commit to a single branch (trunk/main) frequently; enabled by feature flags and CI.
Defining CI/CD pipelines in version-controlled configuration files rather than UI; enables review, testing, and reuse of pipelines.
Deploying services across multiple geographic regions for availability and latency; covers active-active vs active-passive topologies.
Engineering discipline applying software practices to operations (SLOs, error budgets, toil reduction); Google's approach to operations.
Reducing cloud infrastructure costs through right-sizing, reserved instances, spot usage, and FinOps practices.
Testing individual functions or classes in isolation with mocked dependencies; the base of the test pyramid.
Testing interactions between components with real dependencies (database, APIs); middle of the test pyramid.
Testing complete user workflows through the full stack (browser automation, API chains); top of the test pyramid.
Writing tests before implementation in red-green-refactor cycles; distinct from BDD which focuses on behavior specification.
Specifying behavior in natural language (Given/When/Then) that drives test implementation; bridges business and engineering.
Stand-in objects replacing real dependencies in tests (mocks, stubs, spies, fakes); distinct categories with different verification approaches.
Testing invariants with randomly generated inputs (QuickCheck, Hypothesis); finds edge cases example-based tests miss.
Evaluating test quality by introducing code mutations and checking if tests catch them; measures test effectiveness beyond coverage.
Verifying service interactions match agreed-upon contracts (Pact); prevents breaking changes in distributed systems.
Measuring how much code is exercised by tests (line, branch, path coverage); useful metric but not a quality guarantee.
Simulating expected user load to verify system performance meets requirements; distinct from stress testing (beyond expected load).
Testing system behavior under extreme conditions beyond expected load; identifies breaking points and failure modes.
Feeding random or mutated inputs to find crashes and vulnerabilities; distinct from property-based testing which checks invariants.
Comparing output against stored snapshots to detect unintended changes; distinct from visual regression testing (pixel comparison).
Pixel-level comparison of UI screenshots to detect visual regressions; distinct from snapshot testing (serialized output comparison).
Strategy balancing test types: many unit tests, fewer integration tests, fewest E2E tests; the trophy variant emphasizes integration.
Quick tests verifying basic functionality after deployment; confirms the system starts and critical paths work.
Re-running tests to ensure new changes haven't broken existing functionality; the primary purpose of automated test suites.
Verifying software meets business requirements and user expectations; often driven by BDD specifications.
Predefined data and state setup for tests (fixtures, factories, builders); covers setup/teardown patterns and data isolation.
Running automated tests continuously in CI/CD pipelines as part of the development workflow; shift-left philosophy.
Verifying software is usable by people with disabilities (WCAG compliance, screen reader compatibility, keyboard navigation).
Automated security analysis (SAST, DAST, dependency scanning); distinct from manual penetration testing (security domain).
Agreement among distributed nodes on a single value or sequence of values (Paxos, Raft); foundational for replication and coordination.
Selecting a coordinator node in a distributed system; typically part of consensus protocols like Raft.
Copying data across multiple nodes for availability and fault tolerance; covers leader-follower, multi-leader, and leaderless topologies.
Splitting data across multiple nodes by key range or hash; covers strategies and rebalancing but database-specific aspects live in databases.
Hash-based partitioning that minimizes key redistribution when nodes join/leave; uses virtual nodes for balance.
Trade-off theorem: a distributed system can provide at most two of consistency, availability, and partition tolerance simultaneously.
Strongest consistency model where operations appear to take effect instantaneously at some point between invocation and response.
Consistency model preserving cause-and-effect ordering without requiring total order; weaker than linearizability but more available.
Guarantee that all replicas converge to the same value given no new updates; weakest useful consistency model.
Logical clocks tracking causal dependencies between events across nodes; detect concurrent updates for conflict resolution.
Simplest logical clock providing partial ordering of events; insufficient for detecting concurrency (need vector clocks for that).
Data structures that automatically resolve conflicts without coordination (G-Counter, LWW-Register, OR-Set); enable strong eventual consistency.
Peer-to-peer information dissemination inspired by epidemic spreading; used for membership, failure detection, and state propagation.
Transactions spanning multiple services or databases; covers 2PC, 3PC, and alternatives like sagas.
Protocol ensuring all-or-nothing commit across distributed participants; blocking protocol with known limitations.
Minimum number of nodes that must agree for an operation to succeed (W + R > N); tunable consistency via quorum configuration.
Mechanisms for detecting node failures (heartbeats, phi-accrual detector, SWIM); inherently imperfect in asynchronous systems.
Tolerating nodes that may behave arbitrarily or maliciously; requires 3f+1 nodes to tolerate f Byzantine faults.
Scenario where network partition causes multiple nodes to believe they are the leader; requires fencing and quorum mechanisms.
Delivering messages to all nodes in the same order; equivalent to consensus in power and used to implement replicated state machines.
Operations that produce the same result regardless of how many times they are applied; essential for safe retries in distributed systems.
Mechanism for services to find and connect to each other dynamically (Consul, etcd, DNS-based); distinct from API gateway routing.
Synchronizing physical clocks across nodes (NTP, PTP, TrueTime); never perfect, motivating logical clocks as alternatives.
Detecting and fixing stale replicas during read operations; lazy convergence mechanism complementing anti-entropy.
Background process comparing and synchronizing replicas (Merkle tree-based); proactive convergence mechanism.
Sequence of local transactions with compensating actions for failure recovery; alternative to 2PC for long-lived business processes.
Reliable, ordered, connection-oriented transport protocol; covers handshake, flow control, and retransmission but not congestion algorithms.
Connectionless, unreliable transport protocol with minimal overhead; used when speed matters more than reliability.
Application-layer protocol for web communication (methods, headers, status codes); distinct from REST (architectural style using HTTP).
Modern HTTP versions with multiplexing (HTTP/2 over TCP, HTTP/3 over QUIC); covers header compression and server push.
Hierarchical name resolution system mapping domain names to IP addresses; covers record types (A, CNAME, MX) and resolution flow.
Full-duplex communication protocol over a single TCP connection; enables real-time bidirectional data flow between client and server.
UDP-based transport protocol with built-in TLS 1.3 and multiplexing; foundation for HTTP/3.
Translating between private and public IP addresses to conserve address space; covers types (SNAT, DNAT, PAT).
Inter-domain routing protocol exchanging reachability information between autonomous systems; the routing protocol of the Internet.
Network layer addressing (IPv4/IPv6) including subnetting, CIDR notation, and address allocation.
Network traffic filtering based on rules (allow/deny by IP, port, protocol); covers host-based, network, and cloud security groups.
Encrypted tunnels for secure communication over public networks (IPSec, WireGuard, OpenVPN); distinct from TLS which secures individual connections.
Algorithms preventing network overload by adjusting sending rate (AIMD, Cubic, BBR); operates at the transport layer.
Seven-layer reference model for network protocols (physical through application); conceptual framework distinct from TCP/IP's four-layer model.
Protocol for automatic IP address assignment and network configuration; covers lease mechanisms and DORA process.
Server-side proxy handling requests on behalf of backend servers (Nginx, HAProxy); distinct from API gateway (application-level routing).
Data model based on relations (tables) with rows and columns; covers relational algebra foundations and SQL as query language.
Organizing tables to reduce redundancy through normal forms (1NF through BCNF); the opposite of denormalization.
Intentionally adding redundancy for read performance; trade-off between write complexity and read speed.
Data structures accelerating data retrieval (B-tree, hash, GIN, GiST); covers index selection, composite indexes, and maintenance costs.
Internal database components managing how data is stored and retrieved on disk (B-tree vs LSM-tree based engines).
Four properties guaranteeing reliable database transactions (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability); the foundation of relational databases.
Degrees of transaction isolation (read uncommitted through serializable) trading consistency for performance; covers phenomena (dirty reads, phantoms).
Concurrency control maintaining multiple versions of data for non-blocking reads; used by PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, Oracle.
Analyzing and improving query performance via EXPLAIN plans, index usage, and query rewriting; distinct from general profiling.
Combining rows from multiple tables based on related columns; covers join types (inner, outer, cross) and algorithms (nested loop, hash, merge).
Logging changes before applying them to ensure durability and crash recovery; also used for replication (WAL shipping).
Database-specific replication (primary-replica, synchronous/async, WAL shipping); covers read replicas and failover.
Database-level horizontal partitioning across multiple servers; covers shard key selection, cross-shard queries, and resharding.
Dividing a single table into partitions within one database server (range, list, hash); distinct from sharding across servers.
Reusing database connections to reduce overhead (PgBouncer, HikariCP); covers pool sizing, connection lifetime, and health checks.
Precomputed query results stored as tables for fast reads; covers refresh strategies (incremental, full, concurrent).
Searching documents by text content using inverted indices (Elasticsearch, PostgreSQL tsvector); covers tokenization, stemming, and ranking.
Non-relational data models (document, key-value, column-family, wide-column); covers when to choose NoSQL over relational.
Databases optimized for relationship-heavy queries using graph structures (Neo4j, Neptune); distinct from graph data structures (algorithms_data_structures).
Databases optimized for time-stamped data (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB); covers retention policies, downsampling, and continuous queries.
Distinction between transactional (OLTP) and analytical (OLAP) workloads; different optimization strategies and storage formats.
Version-controlled database schema changes (Flyway, Alembic, Rails migrations); covers zero-downtime migrations and rollback strategies.
Server-side database programs (functions, procedures) executed within the database engine; trade-offs with application-level logic.
Automatic database actions fired by INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE events; use sparingly due to hidden complexity.
Database-level deadlock detection and resolution (wait-for graphs, lock timeout); distinct from OS/concurrency-level deadlock concepts.
PostgreSQL-specific but conceptually general: reclaiming space from dead rows left by MVCC; covers autovacuum tuning.
Efficient pagination using opaque cursors or keyset conditions instead of OFFSET; avoids full-scan performance problems.
Protocols for client-database communication (PostgreSQL wire protocol, MySQL protocol, JDBC/ODBC); covers connection lifecycle.
Strategies for database backup and disaster recovery (logical, physical, PITR); covers RPO/RTO trade-offs.
Processing large datasets as bounded, finite jobs (MapReduce, Spark batch); distinct from stream processing (unbounded, continuous).
Continuous processing of unbounded data streams (Kafka Streams, Flink, Spark Streaming); distinct from batch (bounded) processing.
Extract-Transform-Load pattern for moving data between systems; distinct from ELT where transformation happens after loading.
Extract-Load-Transform pattern leveraging destination compute (e.g., dbt in a data warehouse); modern alternative to traditional ETL.
Capturing database changes as events for downstream consumption (Debezium, log-based CDC); bridges databases and streaming.
Centralized repository for storing raw, unprocessed data at any scale; distinct from data warehouses (structured, schema-on-write).
Structured analytical data store optimized for OLAP queries (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift); schema-on-write approach.
Architecture combining data lake flexibility with data warehouse reliability (Delta Lake, Apache Iceberg, Hudi).
Scheduling and managing dependencies between data pipeline tasks (Airflow, Dagster, Prefect); distinct from CI/CD pipelines.
Managing schema changes in data pipelines while maintaining compatibility (Avro, Protobuf schema evolution); distinct from database schema migration.
Storage and transmission formats for data processing (Avro, Parquet, ORC); distinct from API serialization (JSON, protobuf for APIs).
Ensuring data accuracy, completeness, and consistency through automated checks (Great Expectations, dbt tests, Soda).
Tracking data origin, transformations, and consumption across the pipeline; enables impact analysis and debugging.
Organizing data files by partition keys (date, region) for efficient processing; distinct from database partitioning (within a DB server).
Mechanism for slow consumers to signal fast producers to reduce rate; prevents out-of-memory in streaming pipelines.
Ensuring data processing produces correct results despite retries and redelivery; covers exactly-once semantics and dedup strategies.
Centralized metadata inventory for discovering and understanding datasets (Amundsen, DataHub, Apache Atlas).
Handling messages that cannot be processed after retries by routing to a dead-letter queue for inspection and reprocessing.
Processing small batches of streaming data at short intervals; trade-off between true streaming latency and batch efficiency.
Learning from labeled input-output pairs to make predictions; encompasses classification and regression tasks.
Discovering patterns in unlabeled data (clustering, dimensionality reduction, density estimation); distinct from self-supervised and semi-supervised.
Learning through trial-and-error with reward signals (Q-learning, policy gradients, PPO); foundation for RLHF in LLMs.
Predicting discrete categories from features (binary, multi-class, multi-label); distinct from regression (continuous output).
Predicting continuous values from features; covers linear, polynomial, and logistic regression despite its classification use.
Grouping similar data points without labels (k-means, DBSCAN, hierarchical); distinct from classification (supervised).
Reducing feature dimensions while preserving important information (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP); used for visualization and preprocessing.
Creating and selecting input features that improve model performance; the art of representing raw data for ML algorithms.
Metrics and methods for assessing model performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUC, cross-validation); distinct from LLM evaluation.
Optimizing model configuration parameters not learned during training (grid search, random search, Bayesian optimization).
Combining multiple models for better performance (bagging, boosting, stacking); includes random forests and gradient boosting.
Using knowledge from one task/domain to improve performance on another; foundation of modern NLP and CV with pretrained models.
Neural networks using convolution operations for spatial feature extraction; dominant architecture for image tasks.
Neural networks with memory for sequential data (RNN, LSTM, GRU); largely superseded by transformers for NLP but still relevant.
Mechanism allowing models to focus on relevant parts of input (self-attention, multi-head attention); core of transformer architecture.
Models that learn to generate new data (GANs, VAEs, autoregressive models); distinct from discriminative models.
Generative models learning to reverse a noise-adding process (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E); state-of-the-art for image generation.
ML applied to understanding and generating human language; covers tasks like classification, NER, summarization, and translation.
ML applied to understanding visual data (classification, detection, segmentation); distinct from image processing (non-ML transforms).
Breaking text into tokens for model input (BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece); critical for LLM context window management.
Crafting inputs to LLMs for desired outputs (few-shot, chain-of-thought, system prompts); distinct from fine-tuning (weight updates).
Grounding LLM responses with retrieved documents for accuracy and currency; combines retrieval systems with generative models.
LLMs augmented with tools, memory, and planning to autonomously complete tasks; covers ReAct, function calling, and multi-agent systems.
Evaluating LLM quality (benchmarks, human eval, automated grading); distinct from traditional ML metrics (accuracy, F1).
Deploying trained models for real-time or batch inference (TorchServe, Triton, vLLM); covers latency, throughput, and batching.
DevOps practices applied to ML (experiment tracking, model registry, automated retraining, monitoring); the ML equivalent of CI/CD.
Reducing model size and computational cost (quantization, pruning, knowledge distillation); enables deployment on constrained devices.
Detecting and mitigating unfair biases in ML models and data; covers fairness metrics and debiasing techniques.
Understanding why models make specific predictions (SHAP, LIME, attention visualization); required for trust and regulatory compliance.
Artificially expanding training data through transformations (rotation, flipping, synthetic generation); combats overfitting and data scarcity.
Identifying data points that deviate significantly from normal patterns; applications in fraud, security, and system monitoring.
Systems predicting user preferences (collaborative filtering, content-based, hybrid); covers cold start problem and evaluation metrics.
Models processing multiple data types (text, images, audio) jointly; covers vision-language models and cross-modal alignment.
Safety mechanisms controlling AI model outputs (content filtering, output validation, red-teaming); covers alignment and responsible AI.
Building UIs from reusable, composable components (props, state, lifecycle); the fundamental mental model of modern frontend.
Rendering UI entirely in the browser via JavaScript; distinct from SSR (server-rendered HTML) and SSG (pre-built HTML).
Pre-building HTML at build time or request time (SSG/SSR); covers Next.js, Nuxt, Astro approaches to rendering.
Updating static pages after deployment without full rebuild (Next.js ISR, on-demand revalidation); hybrid of SSG and SSR.
Handling navigation in the browser without full page reloads (React Router, Vue Router); covers history API and route matching.
Managing form state, validation, and submission in frontend applications; covers controlled vs uncontrolled approaches.
Reusable UI components, tokens, and guidelines ensuring consistency across products (Storybook, design tokens, brand system).
CSS layout systems and styling approaches (flexbox, grid, responsive design, CSS-in-JS, Tailwind); the visual layer of frontend.
JavaScript build tools and bundlers (Webpack, Vite, esbuild, Turbopack); covers code splitting, tree shaking, and HMR.
Adapting applications for different languages, regions, and cultures (i18n/l10n); covers message formatting, RTL, and date/number localization.
Updating UI immediately before server confirmation, rolling back on failure; improves perceived performance.
Decomposing frontend into independently deployable units owned by different teams (module federation, iframes, web components).
Rendering static HTML with isolated interactive 'islands' that hydrate independently (Astro, Fresh); minimizes client-side JavaScript.
Designing applications to work without network connectivity using service workers, IndexedDB, and sync strategies.
Optimizing web page load and interaction performance (Core Web Vitals, lighthouse, bundle size); covers paint, layout, and scripting costs.
Testing frontend components and interactions (React Testing Library, Vitest, component testing); covers rendering, events, and hooks.
Implementing UI animations and transitions (CSS animations, Framer Motion, GSAP); covers performance implications and motion design.
UI patterns for real-time data display and collaboration (live updates, presence indicators, collaborative editing).
Restructuring existing code without changing external behavior to improve readability, maintainability, or design.
Systematic process of finding and fixing bugs using debuggers, logging, and reasoning; distinct from profiling (performance focus).
Tools automating compilation, linking, and packaging (Make, Gradle, Bazel, CMake); distinct from bundlers (frontend) and CI pipelines.
Managing external library dependencies (npm, pip, cargo); covers versioning (semver), lockfiles, and dependency resolution.
Systematic examination of code changes by peers before merging; covers review best practices and constructive feedback.
Accumulated cost of shortcuts and suboptimal design decisions; covers identification, prioritization, and repayment strategies.
Analyzing source code without execution (linters, type checkers, code analyzers); catches bugs and style issues early.
Team-agreed conventions for code formatting, naming, and organization; enforced by linters and formatters.
Application-level logging practices (log levels, context, structure); distinct from infrastructure-level log aggregation and distributed tracing.
Writing code that anticipates and handles unexpected conditions (assertions, preconditions, fail-fast); broader than security-focused secure coding.
Managing code history and collaboration via version control systems (Git); covers branching, merging, and history management.
Strategies for managing software complexity (abstraction, decomposition, information hiding); covers accidental vs essential complexity.
Writing and maintaining documentation (API docs, READMEs, ADRs, inline comments); covers documentation-as-code and doc generation.
OS-managed execution units sharing process address space; covers creation, joining, and the shared-memory concurrency model.
Lightweight, cooperatively scheduled execution units (goroutines, Kotlin coroutines, Python generators); distinct from OS threads.
Abstractions representing values that will be available later (JavaScript Promises, Java CompletableFuture, Rust Future).
Lock ensuring only one thread accesses a resource at a time; the most basic synchronization primitive.
Synchronization primitive controlling access to a finite number of resources; generalization of mutex (count > 1).
Synchronization primitive allowing threads to wait for a condition to become true; always used with a mutex.
Lock allowing concurrent reads but exclusive writes; improves throughput for read-heavy workloads.
Situation where threads wait indefinitely for resources held by each other; covers four necessary conditions and prevention strategies.
Bug where program behavior depends on uncontrolled timing of concurrent operations; includes data races and TOCTOU.
Threads continuously change state in response to each other without making progress; distinct from deadlock (threads are active, not blocked).
A thread perpetually denied access to resources due to scheduling or priority issues; covers priority inversion.
Language syntax for writing asynchronous code that looks sequential (JS, Python, Rust, C# async/await); built on futures/promises.
Single-threaded loop dispatching events and callbacks (Node.js, browser, Tokio); foundation for non-blocking I/O.
Concurrency model where actors communicate exclusively via asynchronous messages (Erlang, Akka); no shared state.
Concurrency model based on synchronous message passing through channels (Go channels, Clojure core.async); distinct from actor model (async messages).
Pre-allocated pool of threads for executing tasks (Java ExecutorService, .NET ThreadPool); amortizes thread creation cost.
Parallel programming model splitting tasks recursively and joining results; closely related to work-stealing schedulers.
Hardware-supported indivisible operations (CAS, fetch-and-add) enabling lock-free programming; building blocks for lock-free structures.
Data structures guaranteeing progress without locks (lock-free queues, stacks); extremely difficult to implement correctly.
Rules governing visibility of memory operations across threads (happens-before, acquire/release, sequential consistency); distinct from language memory models (programming_languages).
Algorithms designed for parallel execution (parallel merge sort, prefix sum, parallel reduction); covers Amdahl's and Gustafson's laws.
Load-balancing strategy where idle threads steal tasks from busy threads' queues; used in ForkJoinPool and Tokio.
Synchronization point where threads wait until all participants arrive before proceeding; used in phased parallel computations.
